Notes: A 10-year-old boy (referred to as 'Brian' in EC&M article) was electrocuted at a city recreational center pool while buying candy from a vending machine. A damaged power cord from an adjacent soda machine, pinched under the candy machine leg, energized the candy machine cabinet. The broken ground pin prevented the fault from tripping a breaker. The victim was wet from swimming when he touched the machine. All parties reached a confidential settlement. Oklahoma location per ESDPA but not confirmed by EC&M article.
Electrical Source Detail: Damaged power cord from adjacent soda machine pinched under candy machine leg; broken grounding pin on candy machine
Fault Description: Power cord from adjacent machine pinched and damaged under candy machine; broken ground pin allowed cabinet to become energized; wet pool-area floor completed circuit
ESDPA Entry Numbers: FI-XXXX-003 (project file, no date in ESDPA)
ESDPA Data Issues: No date provided in ESDPA document; Oklahoma location cannot be independently verified; EC&M case study matches details but does not specify location
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED location. EC&M case study (2002) matches most details but does not specify Oklahoma or any location. Probably late 1990s or early 2000s. Confidential settlement limited public reporting.

Notes: Two juvenile females jumped into the water off a dock at Lake Travis, Texas, and were electrocuted. Witnesses described them as having 'eyes wide open, mouth wide open, very stiff and unable to talk' before going limp and rolling over. A detective's report noted involvement of a second juvenile witness. The cause was determined to be a lack of grounding system and a faulty battery charger on a houseboat. Referenced in law enforcement training materials by Kevin Ritz (Pacific Coast Harbor Masters) and California DBW/CRLEA training documents.
Electrical Source Detail: Faulty battery charger on houseboat with lack of grounding system
Fault Description: Lack of grounding system and faulty battery charger on a houseboat at dock energized water; breaker likely did not trip despite witness assumption
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED — referenced only in law enforcement training presentations. No independent news coverage found; no victim names or specific dates available. Date likely pre-2010. Detailed witness descriptions suggest a formal investigation was conducted. Could potentially overlap with an ESDPA-listed incident if names/dates were known.

Notes: Diver checking zincs in the Connecticut River at Essex, CT felt strong electric pulses in brackish water. Backed away. Pulses stopped after securing boat power.
Electrical Source Detail: Boat power energizing water; diver checking zincs felt strong electric pulses
Fault Description: Stray current from boat power; pulses stopped after securing boat power
Research Notes: Pre-internet era incident (1981). No independent sources found. Documented only in ESDPA list.

Notes: A 23-year-old woman grabbed a dock cleat to exit the water at Egg Harbor, WI and couldn't let go (tetanic muscle contraction). Her hand went numb. A passerby kicked her hand free. No injuries. An open neutral was found as the cause.
Electrical Source Detail: Open neutral energized dock cleat
Fault Description: Open neutral found; dock cleat energized causing tetanic contraction (can't let go effect)
Research Notes: Pre-internet era incident (1982). No independent sources found. Documented only in ESDPA list.

Notes: Attorney G. Brockett Irwin was electrocuted when his sailboat mast struck an overhead power line crossing Lake O' The Pines near Johnson Creek in East Texas. Three other occupants survived. Brother Tom Irwin is quoted in a 2017 article about the Boy Scout incident at the same lake. After this incident, Upshur Rural Electric Cooperative reportedly raised the power line and installed danger buoys, but clearance issues persisted, leading to the 2017 Boy Scout deaths at the same lake.
All Victims: G. Brockett Irwin (M); Unknown; Unknown; Unknown
Electrical Source Detail: Sailboat mast struck overhead power line crossing Lake O' The Pines near Johnson Creek
Fault Description: Sailboat mast contacted overhead power line operated by Upshur Rural Electric Cooperative; line subsequently raised after this incident
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED LEAD — single secondary source only (2017 article citing this as background). Requires verification through local newspaper archives and court records. ESD-adjacent: overhead power line strike, not dock/marina ESD.

Notes: A 44-year-old man reportedly disappeared while approaching a swim platform on the St. Croix River in Prescott, Wisconsin. A battery charger had faulted and no AC grounding wire was present. The AuGres Maritime Museum compilation suggests a date of June 8, 1986, but no independent primary sources (news, obituaries, official reports) have been found. The incident may predate digital archives.
Electrical Source Detail: Battery charger fault; no AC grounding wire present
Fault Description: Battery charger faulted near swim platform; no AC grounding wire; victim felt tingle then disappeared
ESDPA Entry Numbers: FI-XXXX-002 (project file, no date in ESDPA)
ESDPA Data Issues: No date in ESDPA document; AuGres Maritime Museum suggests June 8, 1986 but unverified
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED — no independent primary sources found. Date of June 8, 1986 is from a secondary ESD compilation (AuGres Maritime Museum citing Boat US). Pre-internet era makes verification difficult.

Notes: Diver drowned at Grosse Pointe Yacht Club on Lake St. Clair, Michigan. No victim name, age, or gender identified. Information relayed third-hand per ESDPA list. Paired with a same-date diver drowning at Petoskey, MI (FI-1986-002); the two may represent a single confused report.
Electrical Source Detail: Diver drowned at yacht club; details relayed third-hand
ESDPA Data Issues: Information relayed third-hand, reducing confidence in details; Sub-incident (A) of ESDPA item #113; sub-incident (B) is FI-1986-002 at Petoskey, MI
Research Notes: No independent sources found despite extensive searching. Pre-internet era (1986) limits available documentation. The third-hand nature of the report and lack of victim details make verification extremely difficult.

Notes: Diver drowned at Petoskey, Michigan, on Little Traverse Bay (Lake Michigan). No victim name, age, or gender identified. Information relayed third-hand per ESDPA list. Paired with a same-date diver drowning at Grosse Pointe Yacht Club, MI (FI-1986-001); the two may represent a single confused report.
Electrical Source Detail: Diver drowned; details relayed third-hand
ESDPA Data Issues: City misspelled as 'Petosky' in ESDPA list; correct spelling is 'Petoskey'; Information relayed third-hand, reducing confidence in details; Sub-incident (B) of ESDPA item #113; sub-incident (A) is FI-1986-001 at Grosse Pointe Yacht Club
Research Notes: No independent sources found despite extensive searching. Pre-internet era (1986) limits available documentation. ESDPA list misspells location as 'Petosky.'

Notes: Detective Jack S. Deuser (41), a 20-year veteran of the Jefferson County Police Department, was electrocuted when he dove into the water at Harrods Creek Boat Harbor to rescue his wife, who was in distress. His wife also died. A faulty light switch and missing ground on a nearby houseboat energized the water. Classified as a line-of-duty death despite being off-duty, because he died attempting a rescue. Survived by three children. Buried at Cave Hill Cemetery, Louisville, KY.
Electrical Source Detail: Faulty light switch and missing ground on a nearby houseboat
Fault Description: Faulty light switch and missing ground on a nearby houseboat energized the water
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as June 8, 1986; correct date is July 29, 1986 per ODMP and Louisville Metro PD; ESDPA location listed as 'Lexington KY'; correct location is Louisville/Jefferson County KY (Harrods Creek); Two dogs survived the incident (noted in ESDPA but not significant for dataset)
Research Notes: ESDPA date (June 8, 1986) and location (Lexington) are both wrong. Multiple official law enforcement memorial sources confirm July 29, 1986, and Louisville/Jefferson County. Wife's name not found in available sources.
All Sources Detail:
Officer Down Memorial Page (memorial) https://www.odmp.org/officer/4045-detective-jack-s-deuser
Louisville Metro Police Department (memorial) https://louisville-police.org/718/Jack-S-Deuser
PSDiver (government) http://www.psdiver.com/images/07-29-1986_Louisville_KY_Jack_S_Deuser_Off_Duty_LEO_Rescue_Attempt_Electrocuted.pdf

Notes: An 18-year-old boy fell off a dock at Bay Haven Marina on Lake Macatawa and drowned. Two would-be rescuers experienced severe electric shock upon entering the water and were unable to complete rescue attempts. The year is uncertain (1987 or 1988).
Electrical Source Detail: Victim fell off dock into electrified water; two rescue attempts thwarted by severe electric shock
ESDPA Data Issues: Year uncertain: listed as '1987 or 1988' in ESDPA; filed under 1987 as default
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Year uncertain per ESDPA list. Pre-internet era limits available documentation. No victim name identified.

Facility Name: State University of New York at Albany (SUNY Albany)
Notes: Bryan Higgins, 20, a sophomore from Mahopac, NY, was electrocuted while wading into Indian Lake on the SUNY Albany campus during a Tau Kappa Epsilon (TKE) fraternity initiation ritual on the evening of April 21, 1988. A faulty underground cable energized the concrete-bottom drainage pond. Two other students (Scott Payne and Vincent Mancino) entered the water to help and were shocked but survived. Higgins was pronounced dead at Albany Medical Center Hospital. Autopsy confirmed cardiac arrest due to electrocution. The university canceled Mayfest and all fraternity pledging.
All Victims: Bryan Higgins (20, M); Scott Payne (M); Vincent Mancino (M)
Legal Outcome: Albany County DA Sol Greenberg determined the death was accidental; no hazing charges filed
Electrical Source Detail: Broken/faulty underground electrical cable near pump house approximately 20 feet from shore
Fault Description: Faulty underground cable energized the concrete-bottom drainage pond (Indian Lake); an electrically powered pump house was approximately 20 feet from shore
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as July 1988; correct date is April 21, 1988 per UPI, NYT, and The Athletic; ESDPA says 'New York State University at Albany'; correct name is 'State University of New York at Albany' (SUNY Albany)
Research Notes: ESDPA date is wrong (July 1988 vs actual April 21, 1988). Five independent sources confirm the corrected date and provide extensive detail. This incident is well-documented due to the fraternity hazing context.
All Sources Detail:
United Press International (news) https://www.upi.com/Archives/1988/04/22/Student-electrocuted-in-pond-hazing-stunt/7322577684800 [1988-04-22]
The New York Times (news) https://www.nytimes.com/1988/04/23/nyregion/student-20-electrocuted-in-albany-campus-pond.html [1988-04-23]
The Athletic (news) https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/330284/2018/04/26/all-heroes-die-but-they-also-live-on-and-on-meet-mahopac-legend-bryan-higgins/ [2018-04-26]
Hank Nuwer Hazing Deaths Database (other) https://www.hanknuwer.com/2008-end-of-year-look-at-fraternal-deaths-and-possible-hazing-incidents-under-investigation-where-death-resulted/
SUNY Albany University Archives (other) https://archives.albany.edu/description/catalog/ua809aspace_1b9a45ef21a43c67342a324c3dc1b350

Notes: Single drowning fatality in Oklahoma from a fault in a submersible pump, freshwater environment. No victim name, age, or specific location identified. The CDC MMWR documented five similar deaths at northeastern Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993) with 96% of docks violating NEC; this 1988 incident likely occurred at the same lake system.
Electrical Source Detail: Fault in submersible pump
Fault Description: Submersible pump electrical fault; likely at northeastern Oklahoma lake system with chronic NEC violations
ESDPA Data Issues: No specific lake or location identified; CDC MMWR report on Oklahoma lake deaths (1989-1993) does not include this 1988 incident, but establishes pattern at same lake system
Research Notes: CDC MMWR report (1989-1993 scope) provides contextual confirmation of the Oklahoma submersible pump hazard pattern but does not cover this specific 1988 incident. No independent source directly confirms this event.

Body of Water: Lake B (southern Oklahoma — lake name anonymized by CDC)
Notes: A 36-year-old male was electrocuted at a lake in southern Oklahoma on June 1, 1989. He was working on a houseboat docked at the lake when his elbow contacted broken-lamp wiring connected to a 110-volt dock power line during a fuel pump installation. CDC MMWR Case 2 (Lake B, southern Oklahoma). Part of a cluster of 5 electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes documented by the CDC (1989-1993).
Electrical Source Detail: Broken-lamp wiring connected to 110-volt dock power line
Fault Description: Victim working on houseboat contacted broken light bulb with exposed energized wiring connected to dock electrical line during fuel pump installation
Research Notes: CDC anonymized lake name as 'Lake B' (southern Oklahoma). Separate from ESD-1989-12-08-1 (Lake A, northeastern Oklahoma, December 8, 1989). Occupational incident during fuel pump installation, not recreational.
All Sources Detail: CDC MMWR (Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report) (government) https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/00042081.htm [1996-07-05]

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 32-year-old male was rewiring a submerged water pump at 'Lake A' (two adjoining lakes in northeastern Oklahoma, likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees) and stringing electrical wire to a relay station. Co-workers found blood on rocks along the shoreline. A lake patrol diver found the victim under 25 feet of water with a laceration above his right eye. CPR was unsuccessful. Medical examiner ruled cause of death as drowning. This was an occupational incident, not recreational. A 1989 inspection of 116 commercial docks at the lake found 96% violated the NEC.
Electrical Source Detail: Victim was rewiring a submerged water pump and stringing electrical wire from pump to relay station
Fault Description: Submersible pump electrical fault; occupational incident during rewiring work
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA describes cause as 'fault in dock wiring'; CDC report clarifies victim was rewiring a submerged water pump (occupational, not recreational); Exact date (December 8, 1989) confirmed by CDC MMWR; ESDPA listed only 'December 1989'
Research Notes: CDC MMWR Case 4 matches this incident. The ESDPA description of 'fault in dock wiring' is misleading; the victim was actively rewiring a submerged pump. This was part of a cluster of 5 electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993).
All Sources Detail: CDC MMWR (Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report) (government) https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/00042081.htm [1995]

Notes: One of four boys in a canoe continued into an electric field near a dock and drowned at Lake Hamilton, Hot Springs, Arkansas. The cause was broken dock wire insulation. Lake Hamilton has been the site of at least three ESD fatalities (1991, 2001, 2008). The pattern of deaths contributed to the passage of Jesse's Law in Arkansas (2011).
Electrical Source Detail: Broken dock wire insulation
Fault Description: Broken insulation on dock wire energized the water near the dock
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as approximate (month only); Some ESDPA versions list date as May 11, 1991 — may be confused with FI-1991-001 (Oklahoma)
Research Notes: KATV article confirms Lake Hamilton as a location with multiple ESD deaths but does not provide specific details about this 1991 incident. No victim name identified. Date uncertainty noted across ESDPA versions.

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 50-year-old man was found lying unconscious on a boat dock at a northeastern Oklahoma lake, wearing wet socks and shoes. His son received an electrical shock while attempting to revive him. An electrical short was detected in the dock wiring. The medical examiner ruled the cause of death as electrocution (not drowning). The victim was found on the dock, not in the water, raising questions about the ESD classification.
Electrical Source Detail: Electrical short detected in dock wiring
Fault Description: Electrical short in dock wiring; victim found on dock wearing wet socks and shoes
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as May 11, 1991; CDC MMWR gives date as July 24, 1991; ESDPA describes 'fault in dock lights energized dock frame'; CDC describes 'electrical short in dock wiring' without mentioning lights; CDC rules cause of death as electrocution (on-dock), not drowning — classification as ESD is questionable
Research Notes: CDC MMWR Case 3 matches this incident. ESDPA date (May 11, 1991) conflicts with CDC date (July 24, 1991). CDC cause of death is electrocution, not drowning. Part of cluster of 5 electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993).
All Sources Detail: CDC MMWR (Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report) (government) https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/00042081.htm [1995]

Notes: Two teenage girls snorkeling near a dock at Alexandria Bay, New York (Thousand Islands region, St. Lawrence River) were paralyzed by electric shock and drowned. The dock wiring had been gnawed by rodents, creating the electrical fault. Secondary sources indicate two bystanders who entered the water to assist were also shocked and hospitalized.
Electrical Source Detail: Dock wiring gnawed by rodents
Fault Description: Rodent damage to dock wiring insulation created electrical fault, energizing water near the dock
ESDPA Data Issues: Date approximate (month only); some sources list August 1993 instead of July; Two bystanders reportedly shocked and hospitalized (per secondary ESD sources) but not noted in primary ESDPA entry
Research Notes: No independent sources found despite extensive searching. The Watertown Daily Times (primary local paper) archives from 1993 are not freely available online. No victim names identified. Date discrepancy between July and August 1993 across ESDPA versions.

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma — lake name anonymized by CDC; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 13-year-old boy jumped from a dock into a northeastern Oklahoma lake while dock lights were illuminated. He immediately surfaced screaming, then submerged and drowned. An electrical short in the dock wiring was transmitting current into the water. This is a textbook ESD mechanism: AC current paralyzed the boy upon water entry. CDC MMWR Case 4 (Lake A, northeastern Oklahoma). Part of a cluster of 5 electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993), where 96% of commercial docks violated the NEC.
Electrical Source Detail: Electrical short in dock wiring transmitting current into water while dock lights illuminated
Fault Description: Electrical short in dock wiring transmitted current into lake water; dock lights were on at time of entry; no GFCI protection
Research Notes: Distinct from ESD-1993-07-00-1 (Alexandria Bay, NY — two teenage girls) and ESD-1993-09-05-1 (Oklahoma scuba diver, submersible pump). Same lake system as ESD-1989-12-08-1 and ESD-1991-07-24-1.
All Sources Detail: CDC MMWR (Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report) (government) https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/00042081.htm [1996-07-05]

Notes: Single drowning fatality in Texas from a fault in a boatlift, salt water environment. No victim name, age, gender, or specific location identified. ESD in salt water is scientifically unusual because salt water's high conductivity typically diverts current around rather than through a swimmer. The cause of death was listed as 'not available' in the ESDPA entry.
Electrical Source Detail: Fault in boatlift
Fault Description: Boatlift electrical fault in salt water environment
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as approximate (month only); some ESDPA versions list 'approximately 1994'; Salt water ESD is scientifically unusual — classification as ESD may be questionable; Cause of death listed as 'not available' in ESDPA
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Salt water classification raises questions about whether this was truly an ESD event. Date uncertain — some versions say September 1993, others approximately 1994.

Body of Water: Lake A (northeastern Oklahoma; likely Grand Lake O' the Cherokees)
Notes: A 46-year-old male scuba diver was electrocuted after contacting a submersible water pump at 'Lake A' in northeastern Oklahoma. A witness reported seeing 'a blue softball-size ball of flame emitting from a pipe' containing power cables near the pump, indicating dramatic exposed and energized wiring. The medical examiner ruled the cause of death as electrocution (not drowning). This was one of five electricity-related deaths at Oklahoma lakes (1989-1993) documented by the CDC.
Electrical Source Detail: Submersible (submergible) water pump with exposed energized wiring
Fault Description: Scuba diver contacted submersible water pump; witness observed 'a blue softball-size ball of flame emitting from a pipe' containing power cables near the pump
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as August 1993; CDC MMWR confirms September 5, 1993; ESDPA classifies as 'drowning' under Electric Shock Drownings section; medical examiner ruled cause of death as electrocution, not drowning
Research Notes: CDC MMWR Case 1 matches this incident. ESDPA date (August 1993) off by one month vs CDC (September 5, 1993). CDC cause of death is electrocution, not drowning as ESDPA classifies it. Part of cluster of 5 deaths at Oklahoma lakes with 96% NEC non-compliance.
All Sources Detail: CDC MMWR (Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report) (government) https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/00042081.htm [1995]

Facility Name: Bolling Air Force Base (now Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling)
Notes: A young boy reached from the water and grabbed the support structure of an electrical junction box at Bolling Air Force Base, Washington, DC, and was electrocuted. Bare energized wires were found touching the metal case inside the junction box, and the ground wire had been intentionally or negligently cut. The base is on the Potomac River with Capital Cove Marina. A second electrocution fatality involving a boy at the same Air Force base is documented (FI-1997-001).
Electrical Source Detail: Bare energized wires touching metal case inside electrical junction box; ground wire intentionally or negligently cut
Fault Description: Young boy reached from the water and grabbed support structure of an electrical junction box with bare wires touching the metal case; ground wire had been cut
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as 'approximately 1994'; an earlier ESDPA version gives 'February 1995'; Victim described only as 'young boy' — no name, age, or other details
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Military base setting may limit public records. Date uncertain — approximately 1994 or February 1995. No victim name identified.

Notes: Single drowning attributed to faults on a boatlift in salt water in Texas, approximately 1994. One of the very few documented ESD incidents in salt water. Could be Gulf Coast or coastal bay area given salt water designation.
Electrical Source Detail: Boatlift electrical fault
Fault Description: Electrical fault on boatlift in salt water
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED — referenced only in ESD incident compilations (Harbor Marine Consultants / Kevin Ritz, AuGres Maritime Museum list #62, 2CoolFishing list #23). No independent news coverage found. Rare salt water ESD case.

Notes: Single drowning fatality at Lake Mead, Nevada, from a fault on a houseboat in a freshwater environment. No victim name or specific location within Lake Mead identified. The ESDPA date of February 1995 conflicts with earlier versions of the same list which record the identical incident as July 1997. Lake Mead is managed by the National Park Service.
Electrical Source Detail: Fault on houseboat
Fault Description: Houseboat electrical fault
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as 'February 1995' in Rev. 8/15/2025; earlier ESDPA versions list 'July 1997' for same description; Date conflict unresolvable without additional sources — may be error in either version
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Critical date uncertainty: Rev. 8/15/2025 says February 1995, but all earlier publicly available ESDPA versions say July 1997 for the identical description. Cannot resolve without additional documentation.

Notes: Seven children behind a houseboat received electric shocks at Jamestown Resort and Marina on Lake Cumberland, Kentucky. No fatalities. A loss of neutral caused the issue. All children survived.
Electrical Source Detail: Loss of neutral caused stray current near houseboat
Fault Description: Loss of neutral on electrical supply
Research Notes: Pre-widespread internet era. No independent sources found. Number of specific victims listed as 7 children; entered as a single Unknown victim entry since individual details are unavailable.

Facility Name: Air Force Base, Washington DC (likely Bolling AFB / Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling)
Notes: A boy walking on ice slipped and grabbed exposed wires on a dock at an Air Force Base in Washington, DC, and was electrocuted. The ESDPA lists the date as July 1997, but this is almost certainly wrong since ice conditions are impossible in Washington, DC in July. The actual month is likely January or another winter month. This is the second fatal electrocution of a boy at the same Air Force base (see also ESD-1994-00-00-1). Likely location is Bolling AFB (Capital Cove Marina area).
Electrical Source Detail: Boy walking on ice slipped and grabbed exposed wires on a dock
Fault Description: Exposed electrical wires on dock
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA lists date as July 1997, but incident involved boy 'walking on ice' — impossible in Washington DC in July; actual date likely a winter month (possibly January 1997); Internal contradiction between date and description is unresolvable without independent sources; Second fatal electrocution of a boy at same Air Force base; see also FI-1994-001
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Critical internal contradiction: 'July' date vs. 'walking on ice' description. Military base setting limits public documentation. No victim name identified.

Notes: Single drowning attributed to a houseboat electrical fault in fresh water at Lake Mead, Nevada, July 1997. Despite Lake Mead being one of America's busiest recreational lakes with major marina infrastructure, this is the only documented ESD incident there. Listed as item #60 in AuGres Maritime Museum compilation and item #22 in 2CoolFishing list.
Electrical Source Detail: Houseboat electrical fault
Fault Description: Electrical fault on houseboat at Lake Mead
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED — referenced only in ESD incident compilations. No independent news coverage or NPS incident report found. No victim name or specific date available.

Notes: A 21-year-old was shocked on a swim ladder while exiting the water at Lake Chelan, WA. He spent 48 hours in the hospital. Rescuers were also shocked.
Electrical Source Detail: Swim ladder energized
Fault Description: Electrical fault energized swim ladder
Research Notes: LOA1 news archive search found this incident referenced in Rifkin/Shafer ESD incident compilation (list item #9, 'CODE VIOLATIONS' category). Still no primary news coverage found. Specific date (July 3, 1998) suggests possible official documentation but none accessible online.

Facility Name: Lake Sonoma Marina (operated by concessionaire for US Army Corps of Engineers)
Notes: Deirdre Mary McCormick, 10, of Windsor, California, was electrocuted while swimming alongside a floating dock at Lake Sonoma Marina on a Saturday in September 1998. She touched a faulty metal conduit; a frayed wire had allowed current to flow through it. She was swimming with her sister and cousin. A rescuer felt electrical current when approaching the dock. The marina was operated by a concessionaire for the US Army Corps of Engineers. Multiple electrical defects were found. The Deirdre McCormick Memorial Play Area ('Deirdre's Park') was established near Warm Springs Dam.
Legal Outcome: Sonoma County DA determined concessionaire was negligent but could not bring criminal charges. Incident prompted California SB 2040 (marina study bill) and subsequent legislation signed by Gov. Gray Davis on Sept 29, 2001 requiring marina electrical inspections.
Electrical Source Detail: Faulty/frayed wire in electrical conduit on floating dock; conduit served gasoline pumps along docks
Fault Description: Frayed wire allowed current to flow through metal conduit on floating dock; multiple electrical defects found on dock
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as 'approximately 1998'; confirmed as September 1998 by multiple sources
Research Notes: Six independent sources confirm this incident. Victim identified as Deirdre Mary McCormick. Initial SFGate report incorrectly states age as 11; all other sources confirm 10. Federal ownership of Lake Sonoma meant state legislation could not directly address this specific marina.
All Sources Detail:

Body of Water: Sacramento River / Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta
Notes: Two boys (ages 8-10) drowned at a marina in Rio Vista, California, on the Sacramento River Delta. Before the fatal drownings, other boys had reported feeling a tingle while in the water and got out. Forty-year-old faulty wiring running under moored boats was identified as the cause, with insulation failure leading to ground faults. The wiring age is consistent with Delta Marina Yacht Harbor (built late 1950s), though the specific marina is unconfirmed.
Electrical Source Detail: Forty-year-old faulty wiring running under moored boats; insulation failure leading to ground faults
Fault Description: Approximately 40-year-old wiring with insulation failure causing ground faults under moored boats
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as approximate (month only); year could be 1998 or 1999; Victim ages listed as '8-10' without specifics; Other boys reported feeling a tingle before the fatal incident
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Year uncertain (1998 or 1999). No victim names identified. The 40-year-old wiring is consistent with Delta Marina Yacht Harbor's construction date (late 1950s).

Notes: A woman was in great distress in 3 feet of freshwater in South Carolina. Her husband attempted to rescue her and drowned. The ESDPA describes this as a 'single drowning' but the narrative suggests two people were affected. It is unclear whether the wife also died or survived. The original research file classified this as 2 dead, but only the husband's drowning is explicitly described in the source material. Fatality count conservatively set to 1 (husband) with wife listed as injured.
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA says 'single drowning' but narrative describes husband drowning during rescue; wife's fate unclear; Source notes 'Dead: 2' but only husband's death is explicitly described; wife may have survived; No specific location within South Carolina identified
Research Notes: No independent sources found. No names, specific location, or cause of electrical fault identified. The 'Dead: 2' in the original FI file may be an inference; ESDPA says 'single drowning.' Conservatively listing 1 fatality.

Notes: A young man swimming toward the stern of a houseboat at Lake Mohave became disabled and drowned. Only 17 VAC was measured behind the stern-drive, demonstrating that extremely low voltages can be lethal in fresh water. The boat had a neutral-ground bond and a homemade shore cord Y adapter that became partly disconnected, causing the hull to become energized. Listed as item #54 in AuGres Maritime Museum compilation.
Electrical Source Detail: Homemade shore cord Y adapter became partly disconnected; boat had improper neutral-ground bond; hull energized
Fault Description: Homemade shore cord Y adapter partly disconnected; neutral-ground bond on boat energized hull; only 17 VAC in water — demonstrates lethal potential of extremely low voltages
Research Notes: UNVERIFIED — referenced in ESD compilations and MikeHolt.com forums (technical details of 17 VAC measurement). No independent news coverage found. Significant for demonstrating ESD at very low voltage. Lake Mohave is on AZ/NV border (part of Lake Mead NRA).

Notes: A young man was disabled and drowned while swimming to the stern of a houseboat at Lake Mohave, Arizona. The cause was a homemade shore cord Y-connector that had disconnected, energizing the water. Lake Mohave is a freshwater reservoir on the Colorado River.
Electrical Source Detail: Homemade shore cord Y-connector that had disconnected
Fault Description: Homemade shore cord Y-connector disconnected, energizing water near stern of houseboat
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Only ESDPA-derived references located. No victim name identified.

Notes: Two boys wearing flotation devices were found face down in the water a few feet behind a houseboat at Center Hill Lake (listed as 'Cedar Hill Lake' in ESDPA), Smithville, Tennessee. The 7-year-old could not be revived and died; the 8-year-old recovered. Electric shock drowning was suspected. The presence of flotation devices indicates shock-induced paralysis rather than inability to swim. The incident reportedly occurred at Sligo Marina (B pier).
Electrical Source Detail: Houseboat electrical fault
Fault Description: Two boys with flotation devices found face down behind a houseboat; electrical fault from houseboat suspected
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA lists lake as 'Cedar Hill Lake'; correct name is 'Center Hill Lake' (US Army Corps of Engineers reservoir); No 'Cedar Hill Lake' exists near Smithville, TN; the actual Cedar Hill Lake is a small lake in Davidson County, ~60 miles away
Research Notes: No independent sources found beyond ESDPA-derived lists. Lake name error: ESDPA says 'Cedar Hill Lake' but correct name is Center Hill Lake (confirmed via Sligo Marina location at 350 Sligo Road, Smithville, TN). No victim names identified. Date of July 18, 1999 from earlier ESDPA version.

Notes: Lucas Ritz, 8, was electrocuted while swimming in the Multnomah Channel near Scappoose, Oregon, wearing a Type II life jacket. His face never entered the water. His mother Sheryl (a graduate nurse) jumped in to rescue him and was paralyzed by the current. His father Kevin performed CPR but Lucas never regained consciousness. The coroner initially ruled the death a drowning; Kevin Ritz investigated, found AC voltage in the water with a voltmeter, and the death certificate was amended to 'electrocuted in water while swimming.' A melted wire on a nearby docked boat had energized the water. Kevin Ritz subsequently co-founded the Electric Shock Drowning Prevention Association (ESDPA) and became an ABYC Certified Marine Master Technician.
Electrical Source Detail: Melted wire on docked boat allowed 120V AC shore power to energize boat's DC ground system (engines, propellers) due to lack of AC safety ground bonding
Voltage: 120V AC (84 VAC measured behind stern per ESDPA)
Fault Description: A 12V wire lying on top of an AC wire melted insulation on both, putting 120V AC into the entire ground system of an un-bonded boat, forcing voltage and current into surrounding water
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as July 1999; correct date is August 1, 1999 per multiple sources; ESDPA describes victim as 'tubing'; multiple sources say he was 'swimming' with life jacket
Research Notes: This is the founding incident of the ESDPA. ESDPA date (July 1999) is wrong; actual date is August 1, 1999. Activity was swimming (not tubing as ESDPA states). Five independent sources provide extensive detail. Kevin Ritz's subsequent advocacy created the modern ESD awareness movement.
All Sources Detail:
Illinois Country Living Magazine (news) https://icl.coop/water-recreation-electric-shock-drowning-lucas-story/
NBC News (news) https://www.nbcnews.com/news/world/grieving-father-fights-invisible-killer-electrical-shock-swimmers-flna866620
Boating Magazine (news) https://boatingmag.com/how-to/electric-shock-drowning-prevention/
Steve D'Antonio Marine Consulting (other) https://stevedmarineconsulting.com/safe-shore-power-and-electrocution-prevention-2/
Electric Shock Drowning Prevention Association (other) https://www.electricshockdrowning.org/

Notes: Two teenage boys were swimming behind a houseboat at Whitman Hollow Marina, Norris Lake, Lafollette, Tennessee. One boy climbed onto the swim platform complaining of feeling a severe shock; the other boy fell back from the ladder — his head was reportedly not below water (possible ventricular fibrillation). He could not be resuscitated. The cause was a damaged power cable to the boat: the black lead energized the hull, the ground wire was burned in two, and the circuit breaker did not trip due to an incorrect connection. The year is uncertain (listed as 2000 in ESDPA; LOA6 LEAD-042 found ESDPA entry #6 listing April 10, 2001 as the date).
Electrical Source Detail: Damaged power cable to houseboat; black lead energized hull, ground wire burned in two; circuit breaker did not trip due to incorrect connection
Fault Description: Damaged power cable to houseboat; black lead energized hull, ground wire burned in two; circuit breaker did not trip due to incorrect connection
ESDPA Data Issues: Year uncertain: listed as '2000 or 2001' in ESDPA; filed under 2000 as default
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Year uncertain per ESDPA list (2000 or 2001). LOA6 LEAD-042 found ESDPA entry #6 (Harbor Marine Consultants 2004 version) listing the date as April 10, 2001 and identifying the specific marina as Whitman Hollow Marina. The mechanism (energized houseboat hull, failed circuit breaker) is virtually identical to the 2012 Cherokee Lake deaths. No victim names identified.

Notes: An owner was paralyzed on the bottom in 5 feet of water while rescuing a dog at the Niagara River, Grand Island, NY. A slight current moved him and he survived. Romex wiring had chafed and energized a steel piling.
Electrical Source Detail: Romex wiring chafed against steel piling, energizing it
Fault Description: Romex wiring (not rated for wet locations) chafed and energized a steel piling
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Romex wiring near water is a serious NEC violation.

Notes: Two young men (ages 21 and 22) died from electric shock drowning after jumping from their dock at Tims Ford Lake, Winchester, Tennessee. A live wire in the water near the dock was found; the cause was improperly installed electrical work on a newly constructed dock. A rescue diver also felt electric shock during recovery. Tennessee subsequently developed new residential dock installation standards.
Electrical Source Detail: Live wire in water near dock; improperly installed electrical work on newly constructed dock
Fault Description: Improperly installed electrical work on a newly constructed dock allowed current to enter the water
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as August 1, 1999; multiple independent sources confirm September 30, 2000; August 1, 1999 date likely confused with the Lucas Ritz ESD death in Portland, Oregon
Research Notes: ESDPA date (August 1, 1999) is wrong. Multiple sources consistently give September 30, 2000. The Aug 1, 1999 date corresponds to the Lucas Ritz death in Oregon, suggesting a mix-up. Filed as FI-1999-001 but corrected date is 2000. No victim names identified.
All Sources Detail:
Mike Holt Enterprises (other) https://www.mikeholt.com/newsletters.php?action=display&letterID=1442
Mike Holt Enterprises (earlier compilation) (other) https://www.mikeholt.com/newsletters.php?action=display&letterID=36
OnTime Service (other) https://ontime59.com/electric-shock-drownings/
Tennessee Injury Law Center (legal) https://www.tennesseeinjurylawcenter.com/water-safety-month-dock-electrocutions/ [2015-05]

Notes: A young bystander disappeared while trying to retrieve sunglasses that went overboard near a Grand Banks 42 powerboat at Put-in-Bay, Ohio (Lake Erie). Electric shock drowning was the cause. A local tourism website references an earlier electrocution 'in front of the Crew's Nest' involving a young man diving for sunglasses and being electrocuted when he grabbed the boat, but provides no date or name. In 2017, the death of Evan Currie at Put-in-Bay was described by WTOL as 'the first electric-shock drowning ever reported in Ohio,' raising questions about whether this 2000 incident was ever officially classified as ESD.
Electrical Source Detail: Fault associated with Grand Banks 42 powerboat
Fault Description: Young bystander entered water to retrieve sunglasses near a Grand Banks 42 and was electrocuted
ESDPA Data Issues: In 2017, WTOL reported the Evan Currie death as 'the first electric-shock drowning ever reported in the state of Ohio' — raising questions about whether this 2000 incident was ever reported/classified as ESD; A local tourism site references an earlier electrocution 'in front of the Crew's Nest' but gives no date or name
Research Notes: No independent primary sources found. Date and all details unverifiable. The 2017 'first ESD in Ohio' reporting creates tension with this entry. No victim name identified.

Notes: A boy was electrocuted while using a conveyor to transfer shrimp in New Orleans, Louisiana. No ground was present on the equipment. This occurred in a salt water / commercial fishing context rather than a recreational marina setting. The ESDPA list classifies it under Electric Shock Drownings, but the description says 'electrocuted' without mentioning drowning. If the victim was a minor working in a family fishing operation, OSHA may not have been notified.
Electrical Source Detail: Ungrounded conveyor used to transfer shrimp
Fault Description: Boy electrocuted while using a conveyor to transfer shrimp; no ground present on equipment
ESDPA Data Issues: Commercial/fishing context rather than recreational marina setting; Description says 'electrocuted' not 'drowned' — may not be ESD in the traditional sense; Victim described as 'a boy' suggesting a minor working in commercial fishing
Research Notes: No independent sources found despite extensive searching (OSHA, NIOSH FACE, news archives, court records). No victim name identified. Commercial/fishing context is atypical for the ESD dataset.

Notes: At Farr Shores on Lake Hamilton, Hot Springs, Arkansas, a girl was shocked after water-sliding into the water at the stern of a boat. A man who attempted to rescue her drowned. The boat had reverse polarity, a neutral-ground fault in a light fixture, and poor bonding to the service. The boat was unplugged in time to save the girl. This was the second of three ESD fatalities at Lake Hamilton (1991, 2001, 2008).
Electrical Source Detail: Reverse polarity, neutral-ground fault in a light fixture on the boat, and poor bonding to the service
Fault Description: Girl shocked after water-sliding into water at stern of boat; reverse polarity and neutral-ground fault on boat; boat was unplugged in time to save the girl
ESDPA Data Issues: Date uncertain: Rev. 8/15/2025 lists June 6, 2001; earlier (2007) ESDPA version lists September 15, 2001; September 15 date may have been swapped with FI-2001-001 (Biloxi) in a later revision; Second of three ESD fatalities at Lake Hamilton (see also 1991 and 2008)
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Date discrepancy between ESDPA versions (June 6, 2001 vs September 15, 2001). SUSP-2001-001 (LOA2) uses the September 15, 2001 date and provides additional detail: reverse polarity, neutral-to-ground fault in a light fixture on the boat, poor bonding to the shore power service; the black (hot) lead energized the hull, and the ground wire burned in two — the circuit breaker did not trip due to an incorrect connection. LEAD-041 (LOA6-C) independently found this same incident. Earlier ESDPA version had more detail about the water slide and light fixture. No victim names identified.

Facility Name: Private dock, Timber Ridge Drive, Montclair community
Notes: Joseph John Salute (15) and Maxwell 'Max' Scott Casillas (11) were electrocuted and drowned at approximately 3:45 PM on June 22, 2001, after jumping into Lake Montclair near a pontoon boat at a private dock. The boat's charging system was crudely rigged: an unapproved dock outlet, a spliced orange utility cord, and the cord plugged into the wrong outlet on the boat sent 117V directly into the aluminum hull. Kyle Fisher (12) also jumped in but escaped with help from a fourth boy, reporting he 'saw a red flash.' Both victims were taken to Potomac Hospital where they were pronounced dead. The lake community does not allow gasoline motors; only electric motors are permitted.
All Victims: Joseph Salute (15, M); Maxwell Casillas (11, M); Kyle Fisher (12, M)
Legal Outcome: Investigators and prosecutors described the incident as a 'tragic accident' that did not rise to the level of a crime; no charges filed. Dock outlet lacked required permit.
Electrical Source Detail: Battery charger on pontoon boat plugged into wrong outlet via spliced orange utility cord, sending 117V directly into aluminum hull
Fault Description: Crudely rigged charging system with unapproved dock outlet and spliced utility cord. Boys plugged cord into wrong outlet on boat (appliance outlet instead of charging outlet), sending 117V directly into aluminum pontoon hull and surrounding water.
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as May 2001; correct date is June 22, 2001 per Washington Post and obituary
Research Notes: ESDPA date (May 2001) is wrong; correct date is June 22, 2001. Three independent sources confirm details. Only documented ESD case in Virginia until 2024. Maxwell Casillas was a student at Saunders Middle School; obituary in The Morning Call (family ties to Allentown, PA).
All Sources Detail:
The Washington Post (news) https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/local/2001/06/23/electrical-accident-kills-two-prince-william-boys/614873ff-46d9-4dd4-9967-3389773c63b2/ [2001-06-23]
The Washington Post (news) https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/local/2001/07/18/electrical-system-blamed-in-drownings-of-2-va-boys/e6c909d4-f838-483a-bc7e-006e2a89f693/ [2001-07-18]
The Morning Call (Allentown, PA) (obituary) https://www.mcall.com/2001/06/26/maxwell-s-casillas/ [2001-06-26]

Facility Name: Bay Marine Boat Works (151 5th St, Biloxi, MS 39530)
Notes: A person was electrocuted at Bay Marine Boat Works in Biloxi, Mississippi. Reverse polarity from recent electrical work was the cause. Multiple people were reportedly shocked. The business (established 1981, owner Paul Bodin) still operates at 151 5th St, Biloxi. The date of September 15, 2001 — four days after the September 11 attacks — may explain the complete absence of media coverage.
Electrical Source Detail: Reverse polarity from recent electrical work
Fault Description: Reverse polarity resulting from recent electrical work at the boatyard
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA lists name as 'Bay Marina Boat Works'; correct business name is 'Bay Marine Boat Works'; Date (September 15, 2001) is 4 days after 9/11, which may explain lack of media coverage; Earlier ESDPA version (2007) listed this date for the Farr Shores/Lake Hamilton incident instead
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Business name is 'Bay Marine Boat Works' (not 'Bay Marina' as in ESDPA). No OSHA records found. No victim name identified. Date may have been swapped with FI-2001-002 (Farr Shores) in an ESDPA revision.

Notes: Interviews with divers in Florida during 2002-2003 revealed many reports of high-level tingle while cleaning boat bottoms. All incidents occurred in salt water with no fatalities. This is an aggregate report, not a single incident.
Electrical Source Detail: AC leakage to through-hull fittings on boats; divers felt high-level tingle while cleaning boat bottoms
Fault Description: Multiple boats with AC leakage to through-hull fittings
ESDPA Data Issues: Aggregate report spanning 2002-2003, not a single incident; No specific dates, locations, or victim names
Research Notes: Aggregate report from diver interviews. A fishing forum reference corroborates reports of divers experiencing tingling. Saltwater conductivity reduces lethality risk compared to freshwater.

Notes: A mother and her adult daughter drowned while swimming from the sundeck of a moored houseboat on Lake Cumberland during Memorial Day weekend 2002. A third daughter survived. The houseboat owner had modified the shore power connection, changing incoming power from 125V to 125/250V and miswiring the ground and hot wire, energizing the aluminum hull, railing, and ladder. Current levels in the water were estimated at 0.01-0.02A, sufficient to cause loss of muscle control in fresh water. The mother could not be saved despite CPR; the daughter was found more than 50 feet below the surface.
Electrical Source Detail: 125V plug at boat end of shore cord rewired by owner for 220V; L2 connected to ground pin; ground lead cut and taped off; hull rose to line potential
Fault Description: Houseboat owner rewired shore power from 125V to 125/250V, miswiring ground and hot wire, energizing aluminum hull, railing, and ladder
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as March 2002; EC&M article says Memorial Day (May 27, 2002); ESDPA detailed list says May 31, 2002; Correct month is May 2002, not March
Research Notes: The EC&M article author is Jim Shafer, who later co-compiled the ESDPA incident list, so this is the same investigator publishing in a professional venue, not a fully independent observer. However, EC&M is an editorially independent trade journal. No other independent sources (news articles, obituaries, court records) were found. The date 'March 2002' in the ESDPA file appears to be a transcription error; both EC&M (Memorial Day) and the ESDPA detailed list (May 31, 2002) point to late May 2002.

Notes: A 14-year-old girl was electrocuted in an apartment complex swimming pool in Arlington, Texas in May 2002. Faulty wiring in underwater pool lighting energized the water. A 16-year-old male who jumped in to rescue her was severely shocked but survived after a third person used a fiberglass shepherd's hook to extract both victims. The CPSC used this case as the lead example in their nationwide pool electrocution safety campaign.
Electrical Source Detail: Faulty wiring in underwater pool lighting fixtures
Fault Description: Wiring problems in underwater pool lights energized pool water; no GFCI protection
Research Notes: Pool electrocution, not dock/marina ESD, but same mechanism (AC current in water). CPSC primary source. No victim names in government record. Not same as ESD-2002-05-00-1 (Lake Cumberland, KY houseboat) or ESD-2002-00-00-1 (Cape Coral FL).

Notes: Three swimmers were in distress near a houseboat at Allatoona Lake, GA. A bystander pulled the shore cord and all three were saved. One person spent days in the hospital. Same lake as a fatal ESD incident.
Electrical Source Detail: Houseboat shore power cord energizing water
Fault Description: Shore power from houseboat energized surrounding water
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Location on 270-mile shoreline of Allatoona Lake not specified.

Notes: A swimmer at Lake Murray, SC reported a strong tingle. Investigation found high-level fault currents in the dock frame. Fresh water environment.
Electrical Source Detail: High-level fault currents found in dock frame
Fault Description: High-level fault currents in dock frame
ESDPA Data Issues: Date listed as 'Fall 2002' — approximate
Research Notes: No independent sources confirmed for this specific incident. An EC&M article about stray voltage at an unnamed lake shares similarities but describes a fatal outcome and cannot be definitively linked. Date is approximate ('Fall 2002').

Notes: Wildlife-only incident. Six ducks were killed when a shore power cord fell into the water as a houseboat pulled away from a dock at Allatoona Lake, Georgia. The 125V plug at the boat end of the shore cord had been rewired by the owner for 220V service, with L2 connected to the ground pin and the ground lead cut and taped off, causing the hull to rise to line potential. No human casualties. Ducks found floating nearby after the fact.
Electrical Source Detail: Shore power cord separated in the middle as houseboat pulled away; 125V plug rewired by owner for 220V with L2 connected to ground pin, ground lead cut and taped off; hull rose to line potential
Fault Description: Shore power cord fell into water as houseboat pulled away from dock; miswired plug energized water
ESDPA Data Issues: Date may be June 2003 rather than May 2003; some ESDPA-derived summaries reference June; Wildlife-only incident: six ducks killed, no human casualties
Research Notes: No independent sources found. This is a wildlife-only incident (no human casualties) unlikely to generate news coverage. All information traces to the ESDPA incident list. Some ESDPA-derived summaries reference June 2003 rather than May 2003; the exact month should be verified against the original ESDPA PDF.

Notes: Two teenage boys were playing on a trampoline in a neighbor's yard when Brandenburg spotted a football in the canal. He jumped in to retrieve it and was electrocuted upon grabbing an electrified boat lift. Kopinetz called for adults and jumped in to rescue his friend but was also electrocuted when he touched the boat lift. Both were pronounced dead at Cape Coral Hospital. Lee County Sheriff's Office investigated. Rebar had penetrated through a power cable to the boat lift, energizing the lift frame in the salt water canal environment.
All Victims: Alex Kopinetz (16, M); Douglas Brandenburg (15, M)
Electrical Source Detail: Rebar had penetrated through a power cable to the boat lift, energizing the lift frame
Fault Description: Rebar penetrated power cable to boat lift, energizing the lift frame in salt water canal environment
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date wrong by nearly a year: listed as May 31, 2002; correct date is May 8, 2003; ESDPA location listed as Cape Coral FL; correct location is North Fort Myers FL
Research Notes: ESDPA date of May 31, 2002 is wrong on both year and day. Orlando Sentinel (May 10, 2003), Brandenburg obituary (died Thursday, May 8, 2003), and Kopinetz obituary all confirm May 8, 2003. May 8, 2003 was indeed a Thursday. Full name of Brandenburg is D.J. 'Bam-Bam' Brandenburg Jr. Full name of Kopinetz is Alex Michael 'Squirly' Kopinetz. Joint funeral held May 13, 2003 at Grace United Methodist Church. Kopinetz family originally from Schuylkill County, PA.
All Sources Detail:
Orlando Sentinel (news) https://www.orlandosentinel.com/2003/05/10/2-teens-electrocuted-in-canal-2/ [2003-05-10]
The News-Press (Fort Myers) via Legacy.com (obituary) https://www.legacy.com/us/obituaries/news-press/name/d-j-brandenburg-obituary?id=39191059 [2003-05-12]
The News-Press (Fort Myers) via Newspapers.com (obituary) https://www.newspapers.com/newspage/213554574/ [2003-05-11]

Notes: A marina manager at Green River Lake found a houseboat with 4 amps of leakage and 8 VAC hull potential after a child reported a tingle. The boat was rewired and leakage was cleared. Same location as another near-miss incident.
Electrical Source Detail: Houseboat with 4 amps of leakage and 8 VAC hull potential; faulty inverter neutral/ground fault
Fault Description: Neutral/ground fault in inverter; deck lights rewired and fault cleared
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Technical measurements (4A leakage, 8 VAC hull potential) represent extremely dangerous conditions — 130x normal safe leakage level. Proactive marina management prevented potential fatality.

Notes: Two women swimming near a rented houseboat at Lake Barkley, Grand Rivers, KY felt strong electric shock. They swam away. A relative confirmed the same experience. Disconnecting shore power resolved the issue.
Electrical Source Detail: Incorrect wiring on rented houseboat caused ground fault via shore power
Fault Description: Incorrect wiring on houseboat; disconnecting shore power resolved issue
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Rental houseboat incident likely handled internally by rental company.

Notes: Two boys were swimming at the bow of a houseboat at Lake Wylie when they called for help. The father of one victim and a friend rushed forward. One boy on the ladder said he was being shocked. The other boy in the water was not moving. The friend rushed aft to pull the shore cord (disconnecting shore power). The father went into the water but his son could not be resuscitated. Wiring errors were apparently done by non-qualified individuals.
Electrical Source Detail: Substantial wiring errors on dock and boat, apparently done by non-qualified individuals
Fault Description: Substantial wiring errors on dock and boat; boy on ladder said he was being shocked; friend in water was not moving
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as August 3, 2003; all verifiable ESDPA list versions date this as June 5, 2004; Date may have been confused with incident #37 (Aug 3, 2004, Lake Travis TX) or #40 (Aug 3, 2003, Bull Shoals AR)
Research Notes: No independent sources found. All information traces to the ESDPA incident list. The ESDPA date in the project file (Aug 3, 2003) does not match any version of the ESDPA list, which consistently dates this incident to June 5, 2004. The date discrepancy suggests a transcription error during extraction.

Notes: A young man disappeared between marina dock sections at Lake Travis, Austin, Texas. Joule marks (electrical burn marks) were found during postmortem examination, and electric shock drowning was suspected as the cause of death.
Fault Description: Young man disappeared between marina dock sections; Joule marks found during postmortem examination
Research Notes: No independent sources found. All information traces to the ESDPA incident list. No victim name, age, or other identifying details beyond 'young man' are available.

Facility Name: Private dock with motorized boat lift
Notes: A 10-year-old boy was electrocuted while swimming near a dock with a motorized boat lift at Lake Waccamaw, North Carolina. His grandfather had raised the boat lift rails about 2 feet above the water to create more swimming space. Children playing in the water reported feeling 'tingles' in their legs before the fatal incident. The grandfather looked into the water and saw the boy floating face down. CPR was unsuccessful. Investigation found the underground feeder cable's equipment ground conductor had been severed at a junction box in the house crawl space, and a water-filled junction box beneath the dock created a current path. The dock was approximately 200 feet from shore and had ceiling fans, a picnic table, and a barbecue grill. The electrical work was unpermitted and performed by an unlicensed contractor.
Electrical Source Detail: Either motor had internal short to casing or water-filled junction box created conductive path between hot and ground wires
Fault Description: Open ground in underground feeder cable at junction box in house crawl space; water-filled junction box beneath dock created path for current; improperly connected bonding conductor
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as June 5, 2004; EC&M technical article references June 19, 2004; Date discrepancy between ESDPA (June 5) and EC&M article (June 19)
Research Notes: ESDPA date of June 5, 2004 conflicts with EC&M article's reference to June 19, 2004. Using the EC&M date as the independent source. The article discusses children aged 8-13 swimming but does not name the victim. The boy's age of 10 comes from the ESDPA file. The EC&M article is the sole independent source but provides detailed technical analysis.

Notes: A diver doing dock maintenance at Saguaro Lake, AZ felt tingling near a pontoon houseboat. The shore cable was disconnected and work resumed without incident.
Electrical Source Detail: Shore cable from pontoon houseboat energizing water near dock
Fault Description: Shore cable from pontoon houseboat caused stray current; disconnecting cable resolved hazard
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Desert lake environment with fresh water. Shore cable disconnect eliminated hazard, confirming fault was in boat electrical system or shore power connection.

Notes: A man was shocked entering the water near boats on generator power on the Sacramento River. Two men jumped in to rescue him; one rescuer was also imperiled. The generator was secured. All survived.
Electrical Source Detail: Mis-wiring on one boat on generator power caused ground fault
Fault Description: Mis-wiring on one boat caused a ground fault introducing current into water
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Generator-powered boats at possible remote anchorage rather than commercial marina.

Notes: A young man in good health was swimming in the evening between two sections of a marina dock at Lake Travis, Austin, Texas. He disappeared under the water and was not recovered until two days later when his body surfaced. The post mortem found no toxic substances. However, the autopsy revealed Joule marks (electrical contact points) on the right wrist and left leg and shin — classic markers of electrical current entry/exit points on skin. The case was listed as suspected electric shock drowning under investigation. The presence of Joule marks at two distinct body locations suggests the body was the conductor between two points of electrical potential — exactly the mechanism of ESD.
Electrical Source Detail: Shore power present at marina dock; victim was swimming between two sections of the dock in the evening
Fault Description: Electrical source not definitively identified; Joule marks at autopsy indicate current entry/exit through victim's body
Research Notes: Found via LOA2 (SUSP-2004-001). Joule marks at autopsy are a rare forensic ESD indicator — most ESD victims show no external signs of electrocution. The ESDPA listed this as 'suspected ESD, accident under investigation.' No subsequent public report of the investigation's conclusion was found. No victim name identified. No independent news coverage located.
All Sources Detail:
ESDPA Incident List (Rev. 5/6/20) (other) https://www.mikeholt.com/files/PDF/Electric_Shock_Drowning_Incident_List_5-6-20.pdf
ESDPA Incident List (Rev. 5/9/16) (other) http://www.electricshockdrowning.org/uploads/4/8/5/6/48564375/esd_incident_list_-_updated_5.9.16.pdf
Harbor Marine Consultants Update (other) https://www.mikeholt.com/files/PDF/Electric_Shock_Drownings_Update.pdf

Notes: Ken Lutrick, 16, was electrocuted at Main Harbor Marina at Ross Barnett Reservoir near Ridgeland, Mississippi. He was with a group of boys after church; they were fishing and then went swimming. When Ken reached for the ladder on a boat that was leaking voltage, the electrified ladder killed him. His mother, Elise Lutrick, co-founded the Electric Shock Drowning Prevention Association (ESDPA) with Kevin Ritz (father of Lucas Ritz, who died from ESD in 1999).
Electrical Source Detail: Homemade shore power cord shorted to houseboat metal siding; boat ladder was electrified and leaking voltage
Fault Description: Homemade shore power cord shorted to houseboat metal siding, energizing the ladder
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as August 8, 2004; mother's account in HuffPost states September 12, 2004; HuffPost article says 'Jackson, Michigan' but Ross Barnett Reservoir is near Jackson, Mississippi
Research Notes: ESDPA date of August 8, 2004 conflicts with mother's account of September 12, 2004. The HuffPost article contains a transcription error listing the location as 'Jackson, Michigan' when Ross Barnett Reservoir is near Jackson, Mississippi. ESDPA describes mechanism as 'homemade shore power cord shorted to houseboat metal siding'; mother's account describes grabbing an electrified ladder on a boat leaking voltage. These likely describe different aspects of the same fault. Full name confirmed as Kenneth 'Ken' Lutrick via CRLEA PDF (LOA1 research, March 2026). A friend in the water also felt a shocking sensation (non-fatal near-miss).
All Sources Detail:
HuffPost (news) https://www.huffpost.com/entry/esd-hidden-danger-in-fres_b_693454 [2010-09]
Trade Only Today (news) http://tradeonlytoday.com/boating-safety/hidden-deadly-danger-in-marina-waters/
Holland Sentinel (news) https://www.hollandsentinel.com/story/news/2010/09/06/group-warns-rare-boating-danger/45298206007/ [2010-09-06]
California CRLEA (government) https://dbw.parks.ca.gov/crlea/Files/Electric-Shock-Drowning-The-Invisible-Killer-CRLEA.pdf

Notes: Tyler Deeds, 22, of Olathe, Kansas, was shocked by a dock cable and fell into the water while helping to remodel a lake house at Lake of the Ozarks, Missouri. He could not be revived. His death directly prompted 2006 code changes at Lake of the Ozarks, though subsequent inspections found approximately 75% of the roughly 25,000 docks did not comply with safety guidelines.
Legal Outcome: Death prompted 2006 code changes governing dock electrical installations at Lake of the Ozarks
Electrical Source Detail: Dock cable shocked victim causing him to fall into the water
Fault Description: Faulty dock electrical cable
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA describes victim as 'stepped on electrical cable while exiting water'; newspapers say he 'was shocked by a dock cable and fell into the water while helping to remodel a lake house'
Research Notes: ESDPA description says he 'stepped on an electrical cable while exiting the water' but newspaper accounts describe him being 'shocked by a dock cable and fell into the water while helping to remodel a lake house.' The newspaper accounts are considered more reliable. Multiple jurisdictions (multiple cities and four counties) at Lake of the Ozarks complicated enforcement of the 2006 code changes.

Notes: A man was badly shocked and paralyzed on a boat ladder at Lake Ontario, NY. Power was turned off and he regained feeling. No ground wire was found in the dock wiring.
Electrical Source Detail: Missing ground wire in dock wiring
Fault Description: No ground wire in dock wiring; boat ladder energized
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Specific date (June 24, 2005) suggests documented incident but no public records accessible.

Notes: Samantha 'Sam' Chipley, 19, of Wilmore, Kentucky, was electrocuted while swimming near a houseboat at Scotts Creek Marina on Cave Run Lake. On June 27, 2005, Chipley and three friends (Margaret, Susie, Courtney) arrived at the marina to spend the night on a houseboat owned by Susie's father. The four jumped into the water despite 'No Swimming' signs. Chipley began jerking in the water. Margaret jumped off a raft to help and felt a shock through her entire body. Another boater dove in to save Chipley, was shocked, and barely escaped. The houseboat's faulty battery charger was energizing the hull. Chipley was pronounced dead June 28, 2005.
Legal Outcome: Samantha Chipley Act (Kentucky HB 356 in 2012, HB 107 in 2014) named after victim
Electrical Source Detail: Faulty battery charger on houseboat caused AC current leakage energizing the hull
Fault Description: Submerged battery charger energized the boat hull; boat lacked proper bonding system
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as September 2004; correct date is June 27-28, 2005 per obituary, news, and BoatTEST; Location spelled variously as Moorhead/Morehead and Scott's Creek/Scotts Creek/Scott Creek
Research Notes: ESDPA date of September 2004 is wrong by about 9 months and in the wrong year. Obituary says died Tuesday June 28, 2005. BoatTEST gives incident date as June 27, 2005. Father: Jeff Chipley. Mother: Roberta Chipley. The Samantha Chipley Act prompted marina industry engagement through AMI and ABYC.

Notes: A diver in Brooklyn, NY complained of tingling and pain. 40 VAC was measured in the water. A neutral-ground short on a recently installed water heater was identified as the cause.
Electrical Source Detail: Recently installed water heater with neutral-ground short
Fault Description: Neutral-ground short on recently installed water heater; possibly bad ground at pedestal
Research Notes: No independent sources found. Location 'Brooklyn NY' is too vague for targeted verification. 40 VAC is a significant voltage.

Facility Name: Private dock with electric boat lift and lighting
Notes: 16-year-old DeSoto HS sophomore died swimming at a private dock in Spring Lake, Jefferson County MO. Three friends were with him; two were rendered unconscious by electrical current and airlifted to St. John's Mercy Medical Center. A fourth teen was present but not seriously injured. Investigation found 10 amps of current in the dock's supply cable, 4-6 VAC measured 2 feet from the swim ladder, and a chain leading into the water with arcing evidence. AmerenUE's 7,200V underground distribution cable had a defective/missing neutral, causing return current to flow through earth and concentrate at the bonded dock ladder.
All Victims: Nicholas Harbison (16, M); Morgan Milfeld (15); Timothy Fitzpatrick (15); Unknown
Legal Outcome: $2.3M jury verdict (2009) against AmerenUE — first stray voltage utility liability verdict in Missouri history; 9-3 jury vote
Electrical Source Detail: Defective/missing neutral on nearby AmerenUE underground 7,200V power distribution cable; neutral current returned via earth to substation; concentrated at bonded swim ladder
Voltage: 7,200V distribution; 4-6 VAC measured 2ft from ladder; 10A in dock supply cable
Fault Description: Chain leading into water showed arcing; dead muskrat and ten dead minnows found at water's edge
ESDPA Data Issues: Victim unnamed in ESDPA list; identified via LOA8 research; Location listed generically; now confirmed as Spring Lake, Summerset Lake subdivision
Research Notes: Victim identity confirmed via LOA8 (obituary/news mining March 2026). St. Louis Post-Dispatch article text recovered via LOA13 (Google Groups archive, not Wayback Machine). Parents: Jerry 'Jay' Harbison and Tina (Curley) Harbison. Buried at Woodlawn Memorial Park, DeSoto MO.

Notes: Jeremy David Whiteside, 24, of Rome, Georgia, was killed on Weiss Lake in Cherokee County, Alabama. He and friends had been wake boarding/skiing on the lake and were returning to shore around 8 p.m. on Sunday, May 21, 2006. Friend Mitch Bowles (of Rome) was swimming to the pier and felt a shock while reaching for the ladder. Thomas Ayer (of Rome) and Whiteside plunged in to rescue Bowles but were shocked within 5 feet of the pier. When the electricity stopped, others pulled Bowles onto the dock. Bowles and another man dragged Whiteside out and administered CPR. Whiteside was pronounced dead at Cherokee Medical Center at 8:40 p.m. Cherokee County Coroner Don Rogers ruled the cause of death as electrocution.
All Victims: Jeremy Whiteside (24, M); Mitch Bowles (M); Thomas Ayer (M)
Electrical Source Detail: Unidentified electrical source at pier; ESDPA mentions electric windmill on dock but not confirmed by independent sources
Fault Description: Pier/dock with unidentified electrical fault; ladder was energized
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as March 18, 2006; correct date is May 21, 2006 per newspaper and obituary; ESDPA mentions 'electric windmill on dock'; not confirmed by independent sources
Research Notes: ESDPA date of March 18, 2006 is wrong by 2 months. Obituary confirms: born Nov 7, 1981 in Floyd County GA; died Sunday May 21, 2006 in Centre, Alabama. Parents: David and Gaynell Whiteside. Brother: Anthony Eric Whiteside. ESDPA-derived lists give May 22, 2006, likely the date of official processing rather than the incident itself.

Northwest Georgia News (breaking report) (news) https://www.northwestgeorgianews.com/rome-man-dies-of-electrocution-after-boating-at-weiss-lak-breaking-new/article_b679d10b-e900-5c62-98e1-791d3399814e.html [2006-05]

Notes: A 56-year-old man was killed while swimming from the stern of a boat at Racine Harbor on Lake Michigan. An inquest ruled the death as accidental electrocution. A reverse polarity light reportedly flickered on and went out when power was applied to the vessel.
Electrical Source Detail: Reverse polarity detected; wife noted reverse polarity light flickered on and went out when power was applied to vessel
Fault Description: Reverse polarity condition; inquest ruled accidental electrocution
Research Notes: No independent sources found despite extensive searching. Local newspaper archives (Racine Journal Times) from 2006 are not digitized or accessible online. Wisconsin DNR boating fatality records from 2006 not publicly searchable.

Notes: Jesse Ryan Fortner, 14, of Saline County, Arkansas, died at approximately 12:30 p.m. on Saturday, June 25, 2006, at Brady Mountain Resort on Lake Ouachita in Royal, Arkansas. Fortner and a friend were swimming near a houseboat dock when both came in contact with electric current from an ungrounded electrical cord. The electricity reached a ladder leading into the water. Fortner's friend was hospitalized at St. Joseph's Medical Center but was expected to be released same day. An unnamed bystander attempting rescue was knocked unconscious. Officials could not definitively determine whether Fortner drowned or died from electrocution. Garland County Sheriff Judy Daniell investigated.
Legal Outcome: Jesse's Law (Arkansas, 2011) — minimum electrical standards for boat docks and marinas requiring compliance with NFPA 303 and NFPA 70
Electrical Source Detail: Ungrounded electrical cord operated by boat owner; electricity reached ladder leading into water
Fault Description: Ungrounded electrical cord with electricity reaching a ladder leading into the water
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as June 10, 2006; KAIT8 news reports Saturday June 25, 2006; ESDPA says Hot Springs AR; more precisely Royal, AR (Garland County); ESDPA says 'faulty shore cord connection'; KAIT8 says 'ungrounded electrical cord'
Research Notes: ESDPA date of June 10, 2006 appears to be off by 15 days; KAIT8 reports Saturday June 25, 2006. Both dates are Saturdays. Full name confirmed as Jesse Ryan Fortner via Hankins & Whittington Funeral Home. His death inspired Jesse's Law (2011), requiring Arkansas docks/marinas to meet NFPA 303 and NFPA 70 standards, though the law has no penalties for non-compliance per KATV 2016 report.
All Sources Detail:

Notes: Three men in a boat at Lake L'Homme Dieu, Douglas County, MN had their propeller catch an extension cord powering a boat lift. They were severely shocked upon entering the water. One was hospitalized for several days (described as face down and unresponsive). All survived.
Electrical Source Detail: Extension cord powering a boat lift caught by boat propeller
Fault Description: Boat propeller caught extension cord powering a boat lift; all three occupants shocked upon entering water
Research Notes: No independent sources found. One victim hospitalized for several days suggests significant electrical injury. Extension cord powering boat lift is a hazardous setup.

Facility Name: River Street Marina, near 10th Street Bridge
Notes: Michael John McNash, 20, of Port Huron Township, MI, died attempting to board a docked boat at River Street Marina near the 10th Street Bridge on the Black River. A faulty shore power cord energized the boat and surrounding water. Despite the main power switch being turned off, electricity continued flowing through the defective cord, and the circuit breaker failed to trip. A Michigan Department of Labor and Economic Growth electrical expert inspected the scene. Police found no criminal wrongdoing. McNash was a 2004 Port Huron Northern High School graduate, multi-sport athlete, and three-summer lifeguard at Lakeside Beach.
Electrical Source Detail: Faulty shore power cord energized boat and surrounding water; main power switch was off but electricity continued flowing through defective cord; circuit breaker failed to trip
Fault Description: Faulty shore power cord; circuit breaker failed to activate; electricity continued flowing with main switch off
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as June 24, 2006; correct date is July 14, 2006 per obituary and newspaper
Research Notes: ESDPA date of June 24 is wrong; correct date is July 14, 2006 (a Friday, confirmed by obituary: 'died unexpectedly as a result of an accident on Friday'). Grandmother Verna Boukamp quoted in Times Herald. Two friends also reportedly shocked but details not fully confirmed in available source text. Brother Patrick H. McNash died August 2, 2025.

Notes: Heather Huff, 24, of Raymore, Missouri, was electrocuted at the 81 Mile Marker of Lake of the Ozarks. She was paralyzed by electric shock when she touched a metal swim ladder on a private dock that was energized. Her father was present and family members pulled her from the water and began CPR immediately, but she was pronounced dead at 5:02 p.m.
Electrical Source Detail: Metal swim ladder on private dock was energized
Fault Description: Swim ladder at private dock was admitting an electric shock when touched
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date of July 24, 2006 is a Monday; LakeExpo article says incident occurred on a Saturday (which would be July 22, 2006); Date requires further verification via Missouri State Highway Patrol records
Research Notes: The LakeExpo article is paywalled and details were obtained from search engine snippets only. There is a day-of-week conflict: ESDPA says July 24 (Monday) but LakeExpo says Saturday (which would be July 22). No obituary for Heather Huff was located online. Further verification needed through Missouri Highway Patrol Water Patrol records.

Notes: Tyler James Howarth, 15, of Gainesville, GA, was electrocuted at his family's lake home on Lake Lanier near Cumming, Forsyth County, Georgia. He was standing in the water working on a personal watercraft beside a metal dock when he apparently grabbed an exposed wire. He fell back and under the dock. Two 15-year-old friends on the dock tried to pull him out but were also shocked. His father arrived, jumped in and pulled his son from the lake and began CPR, but Howarth was pronounced dead at Northeast Georgia Medical Center. He was a student at North Forsyth High School and member of the Raiders Football Team. The Tyler Howarth Community Fund raised over $27,000 for community causes.
All Victims: Tyler Howarth (15, M); Unknown (15, M); Unknown (15, M); Unknown Howarth (M)
Electrical Source Detail: Exposed wire grabbed while standing in water working on a personal watercraft beside a metal dock
Fault Description: Exposed electrical wire at metal dock
ESDPA Data Issues: ESDPA date listed as July 14, 2006; correct date is July 24, 2006 per AP wire story and obituary; ESDPA lists victim age as 17; correct age is 15 per obituary (born Nov 21, 1990); ESDPA lists 1 shocked/survived; actually at least 3 (two friends + father)
Research Notes: ESDPA date of July 14 appears to be a transposition error (14 vs 24). Obituary confirms birth Nov 21, 1990 and death July 24, 2006 (age 15, not 17). Mother: Melody Howarth. Forsyth County Sheriff spokeswoman Karleen Chalker was the source. Annual 5K Run for Tyler held at North Forsyth Middle School.
